g.data-narration: Welcome to Method Overriding in Java - a key concept enabling runtime polymorphism in OOP.
Method overriding allows a subclass to redefine a method from its superclass with its own implementation.
g.text: of a method already defined in its

g.data-narration: This enables runtime polymorphism - the ability of objects to take different forms at runtime.
g.text: at runtime based on actual type

g.data-narration: Four essential rules govern method overriding: same signature, covariant return, access modifier, and restriction on final/static/private methods.
Here's a practical example showing Animal as the superclass with Dog subclass overriding the speak method.
At runtime, the JVM calls the overridden method based on the actual object type, not the reference type.
The @Override annotation is optional but recommended - it verifies at compile time that you're actually overriding a method.
g.text: Optional but recommended! Compiler verifies method actually
overrides a superclass method - catches typos in method name.

g.data-narration: Method overriding provides flexibility, modularity, and easier maintenance in your code architecture.
Methods marked as final, static, or private in the superclass cannot be overridden in Java.
Java allows covariant return types - the overriding method can return a subtype of what the parent returns.
The overriding method cannot reduce visibility - it can only keep the same or increase access level.
Unlike overriding, overloading changes the method signature (parameters) and happens at compile time.
Key takeaways: method overriding enables runtime polymorphism, requires same signature, covariant return, and less restrictive access.
For exams, focus on method signature rules, return types, and identifying correct versus incorrect overriding examples.
g.text: Focus on signature, return type, and access modifiers when analyzing overriding examples.
